course_title: Honours Research Project in Veterinary Technology

learning_outcomes.description: Cultivate and demonstrate the skills, knowledge and attitudes required to perform self-directed, original, independent, ethical research.
Apply skills in communication, time management, problem solving and organisation to meet project milestones and work effectively in a research team.
Objectively analyse current knowledge in your field and use innovative and novel approaches to address gaps in knowledge and/or revise methodologies.
Apply detailed understanding, and demonstrate developing mastery of your research field to evaluate literature and optimise project design, critically and objectively.
Utilise appropriate experimental design and statistical analysis to critically analyse and draw accurate conclusions from your research.
Summarise and communicate your research effectively to a broad scientific audience, in written (thesis presentation) and verbal (seminar presentation) formats.

assessments.due_date:
17/08/2026 - 4/09/2026

The Learning Contract is to be submitted at the time of the Project Proposal Seminar
assessments.task_description:
Students are required to submit a Learning Contract at the time of the Project Proposal Seminar. A Learning Contract template can be obtained from the Course Blackboard site.

The Learning Contract is a Pass/Fail assessment item. This document will be a collaboration between the supervisor(s) and the student within four weeks of commencing study.

The purpose of this document is to ensure, where possible, that the following aspects are addressed before the Census Date:

  • The project has been considered;
  • The supervisor(s)/student responsibilities and study plan have been discussed and agreed upon;
  • Research integrity, budgets, compliance with UQ policies, compliance with external regulatory bodies and funding opportunities have been discussed and understood; 
  • Due diligence is completed; this has been included to ensure the student has every opportunity to succeed in this program.


This is a Pass/Fail item, based on submission of the completed form. Students who are unable to submit the form by the due date will be recommended to withdraw from the program before the census date.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
17/08/2026 - 4/09/2026

A time, date and location will be organised by mutual agreement between student, supervisor(s) and course coordinator for the project proposal seminar presentation within the assessment period.
assessments.task_description:
This Project Proposal Seminar occurs early in the candidature and is designed to allow the student to communicate their proposed research to their peers. It will include an overview of the area of research, a research question or hypothesis, and an outline of the approach that will be used to address this.

The research plan and proposal seminar will be assessed by the Honours Coordinator, and an additional examiner with feedback provided to the student.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
7/06/2027 - 18/06/2027

A mutually agreeable date will be organised between the student, supervisor(s) and course coordinator for the presentation of the Honours Seminar.
assessments.task_description:
20-25 min oral presentation of the student's Honours research project (in-person, Gatton campus)

Seminars should follow the structure of a standard 30min research seminar, with a 20-25min presentation followed by questions from the panel for 5-10 minutes.

Students should seek detailed guidance from their supervisor (s) for the development and review of their seminar.

Students must prepare a seminar abstract suitable for advertising the seminar throughout SVS, have this reviewed by their advisors, and provide the abstract to the course coordinator 2 weeks before their agreed presentation date.

See the SVS BVetTech Honours handbook on the course Blackboard site for further information. A template for the seminar is also available on Blackboard.

This honours seminar is worth 25% of the assessment, and it will be assessed by the thesis examiners or others as appointed by the course coordinator.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This will be an in-person presentation on the Gatton Campus, with an abstract due to the course coordinator TWO (2) weeks before the agreed presentation date.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.due_date:
28/05/2027 2:00 pm

Thesis will be submitted by 2pm of the final day of the second semester of study.
assessments.task_description:
Students will submit a final Honours thesis. The thesis will include an abstract, introduction (detailing previous work and the aims and hypotheses of the current study), methodology, results, discussion (including conclusions) and references. Details on the format and content requirements of the honours thesis are available on the course Blackboard and in the SVS BVetTech honours handbook.

The final thesis will be examined by two examiners – normally, this will be conducted by two internal examiners or one internal examiner and one external to the School of Veterinary Science (SVS). The percentage mark awarded will be the average of the two examiners' marks. Thesis marks will be subject to a moderation process conducted by the course coordinator or a discipline expert nominated by the course coordinator if they differ by 10% or more. A third examiner will be used and the final thesis mark an average of the three marks.

A mark of no less than 50% in the honours thesis is required to pass the course.

An overall mark of no less than 50% is required to pass the course.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Thesis submission is through the TURNITIN system and a link will be provided near to the due date.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
4/06/2027 2:00 pm

Supervisor Evaluation will be submitted by the Primary Supervisor within one week of the thesis due date.
assessments.task_description:
Supervisor(s) will grade students according to their engagement in the research project. This document should be reviewed collaboratively by the supervisor and student at the start of candidature, so both parties understand what will be assessed during the course.

The supervisor(s) mark is an objective assessment of the overall quality of the student in terms of their scholarship, practical competence and professional approach to research.

The supervisor evaluation form is available on Blackboard. The principal supervisor must send the completed evaluations to the course coordinator via email by the due date. Where a student has multiple supervisors who do not agree on a single evaluation, supervisors may submit their evaluations separately to the course coordinator. An average mark from all advisory inputs will be used for grading.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Supervisor Evaluation will be submitted by the Primary Supervisor
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Demonstrate self directed learning

Students will demonstrate self-directed learning by independently identifying relevant learning needs, sourcing appropriate scholarly and professional resources, and engaging with material beyond scheduled teaching activities. This activity supports students to take responsibility for their own learning, manage their time effectively, reflect on gaps in knowledge or skills, and apply new information to veterinary science, clinical decision-making, research or professional practice contexts.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Demonstrate self directed learning Students will demonstrate self-directed learning by independently identifying relevant learning needs, sourcing appropriate scholarly and professional resources, and engaging with material beyond scheduled teaching activities. This activity supports students to take responsibility for their own learning, manage their time effectively, reflect on gaps in knowledge or skills, and apply new information to veterinary science, clinical decision-making, research or professional practice contexts.

learning_activities.topic:
Review and critique published scientific literature

Students will review and critique published scientific literature relevant to veterinary science and animal health. This activity will develop students’ ability to read research papers critically, identify the strengths and limitations of study design, interpret results, assess the quality of evidence, and consider how research findings may be applied in clinical, industry or research contexts. Students will be expected to discuss key findings, evaluate the validity of conclusions, and reflect on the relevance of the literature to evidence-based veterinary practice.

Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L06
learning_activities.topic_title: Review and critique published scientific literature
learning_activities.topic_description: Review and critique published scientific literature Students will review and critique published scientific literature relevant to veterinary science and animal health. This activity will develop students’ ability to read research papers critically, identify the strengths and limitations of study design, interpret results, assess the quality of evidence, and consider how research findings may be applied in clinical, industry or research contexts. Students will be expected to discuss key findings, evaluate the validity of conclusions, and reflect on the relevance of the literature to evidence-based veterinary practice.

learning_activities.topic:
Devise, plan and conduct an Honours research project

Students will devise, plan and conduct an Honours research project under academic supervision. This activity will develop students’ capacity to formulate a research question, design an appropriate methodology, consider ethical and practical requirements, collect and manage data, analyse findings, and interpret results in relation to the existing literature. Students will progressively build research independence, problem-solving skills, scientific judgement and professional communication skills through the completion of a substantial research project.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_title: Devise, plan and conduct an Honours research project
learning_activities.topic_description: Devise, plan and conduct an Honours research project Students will devise, plan and conduct an Honours research project under academic supervision. This activity will develop students’ capacity to formulate a research question, design an appropriate methodology, consider ethical and practical requirements, collect and manage data, analyse findings, and interpret results in relation to the existing literature. Students will progressively build research independence, problem-solving skills, scientific judgement and professional communication skills through the completion of a substantial research project.

learning_activities.topic:
Develop research literacy through participation in research seminars

Students will develop research literacy through participation in research seminars delivered by academic staff, researchers, peers and external experts. This activity will expose students to a range of research questions, methodologies, analytical approaches and disciplinary perspectives relevant to veterinary science. Through active listening, questioning and reflection, students will build their ability to interpret research presentations, evaluate evidence, recognise features of effective scientific communication, and situate their own Honours research within the broader research landscape.

Learning outcomes: L06
learning_activities.topic_title: Develop research literacy through participation in research seminars
learning_activities.topic_description: Develop research literacy through participation in research seminars Students will develop research literacy through participation in research seminars delivered by academic staff, researchers, peers and external experts. Through active listening, questioning and reflection, students will build their ability to interpret research presentations, evaluate evidence, recognise features of effective scientific communication, and situate their own Honours research within the broader research landscape.

learning_activities.topic:
Work in a laboratory environment (optional)

Students may have the opportunity to work in a laboratory environment as part of their Honours research project, where this is relevant to the project design. This activity will support the development of practical research skills, including safe laboratory practice, appropriate use of equipment, sample handling, data collection, record keeping, and adherence to relevant ethical, biosafety and workplace health and safety requirements. Students will develop an understanding of how laboratory methods contribute to research quality, reliability and interpretation of results.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Work in a laboratory environment (optional) Students may have the opportunity to work in a laboratory environment as part of their Honours research project, where this is relevant to the project design. This activity will support the development of practical research skills, including safe laboratory practice, appropriate use of equipment, sample handling, data collection, record keeping, and adherence to relevant ethical, biosafety and workplace health and safety requirements. Students will develop an understanding of how laboratory methods contribute to research quality, reliability and interpretation of results.

learning_activities.topic:
Collect field based data (optional)

Students may have the opportunity to collect field-based data as part of their Honours research project, where this is relevant to the project design. This activity will support the development of practical research skills in real-world veterinary, animal production, wildlife, clinical or industry settings. Students will develop skills in planning fieldwork, collecting accurate and reliable data, maintaining appropriate records, managing samples or observations, and working safely and professionally in variable field conditions. The activity will also reinforce the importance of ethical approval, biosecurity, animal welfare, workplace health and safety, and data quality in applied research.

Learning outcomes: L01
